name: Nat
const.docs:
The natural numbers, starting at zero.

This type is special-cased by both the kernel and the compiler, and overridden with an efficient
implementation. Both use a fast arbitrary-precision arithmetic library (usually
[GMP](https://gmplib.org/)); at runtime, `Nat` values that are sufficiently small are unboxed.

name: Lean
Parser
Command
namespace
keyword.docs:
`namespace <id>` opens a section with label `<id>` that influences naming and name resolution inside
the section:
* Declarations names are prefixed: `def seventeen : ÔäĽ := 17` inside a namespace `Nat` is given the
  full name `Nat.seventeen`.
* Names introduced by `export` declarations are also prefixed by the identifier.
* All names starting with `<id>.` become available in the namespace without the prefix. These names
  are preferred over names introduced by outer namespaces or `open`.
* Within a namespace, declarations can be `protected`, which excludes them from the effects of
  opening the namespace.

As with `section`, namespaces can be nested and the scope of a namespace is terminated by a
corresponding `end <id>` or the end of the file.

`namespace` also acts like `section` in delimiting the scope of `variable`, `open`, and other scoped commands.

sort.doc?: A specific universe in Lean's infinite hierarchy of universes.

name: Lean
Parser
Command
end
keyword.docs:
`end` closes a `section` or `namespace` scope. If the scope is named `<id>`, it has to be closed
with `end <id>`. The `end` command is optional at the end of a file.

name: Lean
Parser
Term
sorry
keyword.docs:
The `sorry` term is a temporary placeholder for a missing proof or value.

The syntax is intended for stubbing-out incomplete parts of a value or proof while still having a syntactically correct skeleton.
Lean will give a warning whenever a declaration uses `sorry`, so you aren't likely to miss it,
but you can double check if a declaration depends on `sorry` by looking for `sorryAx` in the output
of the `#print axioms my_thm` command, the axiom used by the implementation of `sorry`.

"Go to definition" on `sorry` in the Infoview will go to the source position where it was introduced, if such information is available.

Each `sorry` is guaranteed to be unique, so for example the following fails:
```lean
example : (sorry : Nat) = sorry := rfl -- fails
```

See also the `sorry` tactic, which is short for `exact sorry`.

Nor
intro
Lean
Parser
Command
inductive
keyword.docs:
In Lean, every concrete type other than the universes
and every type constructor other than dependent arrows
is an instance of a general family of type constructions known as inductive types.
It is remarkable that it is possible to construct a substantial edifice of mathematics
based on nothing more than the type universes, dependent arrow types, and inductive types;
everything else follows from those.
Intuitively, an inductive type is built up from a specified list of constructors.
For example, `List ╬▒` is the list of elements of type `╬▒`, and is defined as follows:
```
inductive List (╬▒ : Type u) where
| nil
| cons (head : ╬▒) (tail : List ╬▒)
```
A list of elements of type `╬▒` is either the empty list, `nil`,
or an element `head : ╬▒` followed by a list `tail : List ╬▒`.
See [Inductive types](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/inductive_types.html)
for more information.

name: False
const.docs:
`False` is the empty proposition. Thus, it has no introduction rules.
It represents a contradiction. `False` elimination rule, `False.rec`,
expresses the fact that anything follows from a contradiction.
This rule is sometimes called ex falso (short for ex falso sequitur quodlibet),
or the principle of explosion.
For more information: [Propositional Logic](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/propositions_and_proofs.html#propositional-logic)

name: False
elim
const.docs:
`False.elim : False Ôćĺ C` says that from `False`, any desired proposition
`C` holds. Also known as ex falso quodlibet (EFQ) or the principle of explosion.

The target type is actually `C : Sort u` which means it works for both
propositions and types. When executed, this acts like an "unreachable"
instruction: it is **undefined behavior** to run, but it will probably print
"unreachable code". (You would need to construct a proof of false to run it
anyway, which you can only do using `sorry` or unsound axioms.)

name: Lean
Parser
Command
end
keyword.docs:
`end` closes a `section` or `namespace` scope. If the scope is named `<id>`, it has to be closed
with `end <id>`. The `end` command is optional at the end of a file.

anonCtor.signature: Iff.intro {a b : Prop} (mp : a Ôćĺ b) (mpr : b Ôćĺ a) : a Ôćö b
name: Iff
intro
anonCtor.docs: If `a Ôćĺ b` and `b Ôćĺ a` then `a` and `b` are equivalent.

anonCtor.signature: Iff.intro {a b : Prop} (mp : a Ôćĺ b) (mpr : b Ôćĺ a) : a Ôćö b
name: Iff
intro
anonCtor.docs: If `a Ôćĺ b` and `b Ôćĺ a` then `a` and `b` are equivalent.

name: Iff
mpr
const.docs: Modus ponens for if and only if, reversed. If `a Ôćö b` and `b`, then `a`.

name: Iff
mp
const.docs: Modus ponens for if and only if. If `a Ôćö b` and `a`, then `b`.

name: id
const.docs:
The identity function. `id` takes an implicit argument `╬▒ : Sort u`
(a type in any universe), and an argument `a : ╬▒`, and returns `a`.

Although this may look like a useless function, one application of the identity
function is to explicitly put a type on an expression. If `e` has type `T`,
and `T'` is definitionally equal to `T`, then `@id T' e` typechecks, and Lean
knows that this expression has type `T'` rather than `T`. This can make a
difference for typeclass inference, since `T` and `T'` may have different
typeclass instances on them. `show T' from e` is sugar for an `@id T' e`
expression.

const.signature: Iff.intro {a b : Prop} (mp : a Ôćĺ b) (mpr : b Ôćĺ a) : a Ôćö b
name: Iff
intro
const.docs: If `a Ôćĺ b` and `b Ôćĺ a` then `a` and `b` are equivalent.

name: Lean
Parser
Term
nomatch
keyword.docs:
Empty match/ex falso. `nomatch e` is of arbitrary type `╬▒ : Sort u` if
Lean can show that an empty set of patterns is exhaustive given `e`'s type,
e.g. because it has no constructors.

name: Or
inl
const.docs: `Or.inl` is "left injection" into an `Or`. If `h : a` then `Or.inl h : a ÔłĘ b`.

name: trivial

`True` is true, and `True.intro` (or more commonly, `trivial`)
is the proof.

name: True
intro
const.docs:
`True` is true, and `True.intro` (or more commonly, `trivial`)
is the proof.

name: True
const.docs:
`True` is a proposition and has only an introduction rule, `True.intro : True`.
In other words, `True` is simply true, and has a canonical proof, `True.intro`
For more information: [Propositional Logic](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/propositions_and_proofs.html#propositional-logic)

Temp

Lean
Parser
Term
match
keyword.docs:
Pattern matching. `match e, ... with | p, ... => f | ...` matches each given
term `e` against each pattern `p` of a match alternative. When all patterns
of an alternative match, the `match` term evaluates to the value of the
corresponding right-hand side `f` with the pattern variables bound to the
respective matched values.
If used as `match h : e, ... with | p, ... => f | ...`, `h : e = p` is available
within `f`.

When not constructing a proof, `match` does not automatically substitute variables
matched on in dependent variables' types. Use `match (generalizing := true) ...` to
enforce this.

Syntax quotations can also be used in a pattern match.
This matches a `Syntax` value against quotations, pattern variables, or `_`.

Quoted identifiers only match identical identifiers - custom matching such as by the preresolved
names only should be done explicitly.

`Syntax.atom`s are ignored during matching by default except when part of a built-in literal.
For users introducing new atoms, we recommend wrapping them in dedicated syntax kinds if they
should participate in matching.
For example, in
```lean
syntax "c" ("foo" <|> "bar") ...
```
`foo` and `bar` are indistinguishable during matching, but in
```lean
syntax foo := "foo"
syntax "c" (foo <|> "bar") ...
```
they are not.

Lean
Parser
Command
inductive
keyword.docs:
In Lean, every concrete type other than the universes
and every type constructor other than dependent arrows
is an instance of a general family of type constructions known as inductive types.
It is remarkable that it is possible to construct a substantial edifice of mathematics
based on nothing more than the type universes, dependent arrow types, and inductive types;
everything else follows from those.
Intuitively, an inductive type is built up from a specified list of constructors.
For example, `List ╬▒` is the list of elements of type `╬▒`, and is defined as follows:
```
inductive List (╬▒ : Type u) where
| nil
| cons (head : ╬▒) (tail : List ╬▒)
```
A list of elements of type `╬▒` is either the empty list, `nil`,
or an element `head : ╬▒` followed by a list `tail : List ╬▒`.
See [Inductive types](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/inductive_types.html)
for more information.

name: Lean
Parser
Command
namespace
keyword.docs:
`namespace <id>` opens a section with label `<id>` that influences naming and name resolution inside
the section:
* Declarations names are prefixed: `def seventeen : ÔäĽ := 17` inside a namespace `Nat` is given the
  full name `Nat.seventeen`.
* Names introduced by `export` declarations are also prefixed by the identifier.
* All names starting with `<id>.` become available in the namespace without the prefix. These names
  are preferred over names introduced by outer namespaces or `open`.
* Within a namespace, declarations can be `protected`, which excludes them from the effects of
  opening the namespace.

As with `section`, namespaces can be nested and the scope of a namespace is terminated by a
corresponding `end <id>` or the end of the file.

`namespace` also acts like `section` in delimiting the scope of `variable`, `open`, and other scoped commands.

name: Lean
Parser
Term
sorry
keyword.docs:
The `sorry` term is a temporary placeholder for a missing proof or value.

The syntax is intended for stubbing-out incomplete parts of a value or proof while still having a syntactically correct skeleton.
Lean will give a warning whenever a declaration uses `sorry`, so you aren't likely to miss it,
but you can double check if a declaration depends on `sorry` by looking for `sorryAx` in the output
of the `#print axioms my_thm` command, the axiom used by the implementation of `sorry`.

"Go to definition" on `sorry` in the Infoview will go to the source position where it was introduced, if such information is available.

Each `sorry` is guaranteed to be unique, so for example the following fails:
```lean
example : (sorry : Nat) = sorry := rfl -- fails
```

See also the `sorry` tactic, which is short for `exact sorry`.

const.docs:
Extract the right conjunct from a conjunction. `h : a Ôłž b` then
`h.right`, also notated as `h.2`, is a proof of `b`.

sort.doc?:
The universe of propositions. `Prop Ôëí Sort 0`.

Every proposition is propositionally equal to either `True` or `False`.

Extract the left conjunct from a conjunction. `h : a Ôłž b` then
`h.left`, also notated as `h.1`, is a proof of `a`.

left
const.docs:
Extract the left conjunct from a conjunction. `h : a Ôłž b` then
`h.left`, also notated as `h.1`, is a proof of `a`.

right
const.docs:
Extract the right conjunct from a conjunction. `h : a Ôłž b` then
`h.right`, also notated as `h.2`, is a proof of `b`.

const.signature: And.intro {a b : Prop} (left : a) (right : b) : a Ôłž b
name: And
intro
const.docs: `And.intro : a Ôćĺ b Ôćĺ a Ôłž b` is the constructor for the And operation.

name: And
const.docs:
`And a b`, or `a Ôłž b`, is the conjunction of propositions. It can be
constructed and destructed like a pair: if `ha : a` and `hb : b` then
`ÔčĘha, hbÔčę : a Ôłž b`, and if `h : a Ôłž b` then `h.left : a` and `h.right : b`.


Conventions for notations in identifiers:

 * The recommended spelling of `Ôłž` in identifiers is `and`.

 * The recommended spelling of `/\` in identifiers is `and` (prefer `Ôłž` over `/\`).

name: Lean
Parser
Command
variable
keyword.docs:
Declares one or more typed variables, or modifies whether already-declared variables are
  implicit.

Introduces variables that can be used in definitions within the same `namespace` or `section` block.
When a definition mentions a variable, Lean will add it as an argument of the definition. This is
useful in particular when writing many definitions that have parameters in common (see below for an
example).

Variable declarations have the same flexibility as regular function parameters. In particular they
can be [explicit, implicit][binder docs], or [instance implicit][tpil classes] (in which case they
can be anonymous). This can be changed, for instance one can turn explicit variable `x` into an
implicit one with `variable {x}`. Note that currently, you should avoid changing how variables are
bound and declare new variables at the same time; see [issue 2789] for more on this topic.

In *theorem bodies* (i.e. proofs), variables are not included based on usage in order to ensure that
changes to the proof cannot change the statement of the overall theorem. Instead, variables are only
available to the proof if they have been mentioned in the theorem header or in an `include` command
or are instance implicit and depend only on such variables.

See [*Variables and Sections* from Theorem Proving in Lean][tpil vars] for a more detailed
discussion.

[tpil vars]:
https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/dependent_type_theory.html#variables-and-sections
(Variables and Sections on Theorem Proving in Lean) [tpil classes]:
https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/type_classes.html (Type classes on Theorem Proving in
Lean) [binder docs]:
https://leanprover-community.github.io/mathlib4_docs/Lean/Expr.html#Lean.BinderInfo (Documentation
for the BinderInfo type) [issue 2789]: https://github.com/leanprover/lean4/issues/2789 (Issue 2789
on github)

## Examples

```lean
section
  variable
    {╬▒ : Type u}      -- implicit
    (a : ╬▒)           -- explicit
    [instBEq : BEq ╬▒] -- instance implicit, named
    [Hashable ╬▒]      -- instance implicit, anonymous

  def isEqual (b : ╬▒) : Bool :=
    a == b

  #check isEqual
  -- isEqual.{u} {╬▒ : Type u} (a : ╬▒) [instBEq : BEq ╬▒] (b : ╬▒) : Bool

  variable
    {a} -- `a` is implicit now

  def eqComm {b : ╬▒} := a == b Ôćö b == a

  #check eqComm
  -- eqComm.{u} {╬▒ : Type u} {a : ╬▒} [instBEq : BEq ╬▒] {b : ╬▒} : Prop
end
```

The following shows a typical use of `variable` to factor out definition arguments:

```lean
variable (Src : Type)

structure Logger where
  trace : List (Src ├Ś String)
#check Logger
-- Logger (Src : Type) : Type

namespace Logger
  -- switch `Src : Type` to be implicit until the `end Logger`
  variable {Src}

  def empty : Logger Src where
    trace := []
  #check empty
  -- Logger.empty {Src : Type} : Logger Src

  variable (log : Logger Src)

  def len :=
    log.trace.length
  #check len
  -- Logger.len {Src : Type} (log : Logger Src) : Nat

  variable (src : Src) [BEq Src]

  -- at this point all of `log`, `src`, `Src` and the `BEq` instance can all become arguments

  def filterSrc :=
    log.trace.filterMap
      fun (src', str') => if src' == src then some str' else none
  #check filterSrc
  -- Logger.filterSrc {Src : Type} (log : Logger Src) (src : Src) [instÔťŁ : BEq Src] : List String

  def lenSrc :=
    log.filterSrc src |>.length
  #check lenSrc
  -- Logger.lenSrc {Src : Type} (log : Logger Src) (src : Src) [instÔťŁ : BEq Src] : Nat
end Logger
```

The following example demonstrates availability of variables in proofs:
```lean
variable
  {╬▒ : Type}    -- available in the proof as indirectly mentioned through `a`
  [ToString ╬▒]  -- available in the proof as `╬▒` is included
  (a : ╬▒)       -- available in the proof as mentioned in the header
  {╬▓ : Type}    -- not available in the proof
  [ToString ╬▓]  -- not available in the proof

theorem ex : a = a := rfl
```
After elaboration of the proof, the following warning will be generated to highlight the unused
hypothesis:
```
included section variable '[ToString ╬▒]' is not used in 'ex', consider excluding it
```
In such cases, the offending variable declaration should be moved down or into a section so that
only theorems that do depend on it follow it until the end of the section.

name: Lean
Parser
Command
end
keyword.docs:
`end` closes a `section` or `namespace` scope. If the scope is named `<id>`, it has to be closed
with `end <id>`. The `end` command is optional at the end of a file.

name: Lean
Parser
Term
match
keyword.docs:
Pattern matching. `match e, ... with | p, ... => f | ...` matches each given
term `e` against each pattern `p` of a match alternative. When all patterns
of an alternative match, the `match` term evaluates to the value of the
corresponding right-hand side `f` with the pattern variables bound to the
respective matched values.
If used as `match h : e, ... with | p, ... => f | ...`, `h : e = p` is available
within `f`.

When not constructing a proof, `match` does not automatically substitute variables
matched on in dependent variables' types. Use `match (generalizing := true) ...` to
enforce this.

Syntax quotations can also be used in a pattern match.
This matches a `Syntax` value against quotations, pattern variables, or `_`.

Quoted identifiers only match identical identifiers - custom matching such as by the preresolved
names only should be done explicitly.

`Syntax.atom`s are ignored during matching by default except when part of a built-in literal.
For users introducing new atoms, we recommend wrapping them in dedicated syntax kinds if they
should participate in matching.
For example, in
```lean
syntax "c" ("foo" <|> "bar") ...
```
`foo` and `bar` are indistinguishable during matching, but in
```lean
syntax foo := "foo"
syntax "c" (foo <|> "bar") ...
```
they are not.

keyword.docs:
Declares one or more typed variables, or modifies whether already-declared variables are
  implicit.

Introduces variables that can be used in definitions within the same `namespace` or `section` block.
When a definition mentions a variable, Lean will add it as an argument of the definition. This is

[tpil vars]:
https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/dependent_type_theory.html#variables-and-sections
(Variables and Sections on Theorem Proving in Lean) [tpil classes]:
https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/type_classes.html (Type classes on Theorem Proving in

for the BinderInfo type) [issue 2789]: https://github.com/leanprover/lean4/issues/2789 (Issue 2789
on github)

## Examples

```lean

  variable
    {╬▒ : Type u}      -- implicit
    (a : ╬▒)           -- explicit
    [instBEq : BEq ╬▒] -- instance implicit, named
    [Hashable ╬▒]      -- instance implicit, anonymous

  -- isEqual.{u} {╬▒ : Type u} (a : ╬▒) [instBEq : BEq ╬▒] (b : ╬▒) : Bool

    {a} -- `a` is implicit now

  def eqComm {b : ╬▒} := a == b Ôćö b == a
```

The following shows a typical use of `variable` to factor out definition arguments:

```lean

#check Logger

      fun (src', str') => if src' == src then some str' else none

  def lenSrc :=
    log.filterSrc src |>.length
  #check lenSrc
  -- Logger.lenSrc {Src : Type} (log : Logger Src) (src : Src) [instÔťŁ : BEq Src] : Nat
end Logger
```

The following example demonstrates availability of variables in proofs:
```lean

  {╬▒ : Type}    -- available in the proof as indirectly mentioned through `a`
  [ToString ╬▒]  -- available in the proof as `╬▒` is included
```

only theorems that do depend on it follow it until the end of the section.

inductive
keyword.docs:

Each `sorry` is guaranteed to be unique, so for example the following fails:
```lean
example : (sorry : Nat) = sorry := rfl -- fails
```

See also the `sorry` tactic, which is short for `exact sorry`.

144.text: This linter can be disabled with `set_option

text.str: /- Finally, example is not just for `Prop`: -/
--unhide

References
===

- Section 7.3 of [TPL](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/inductive_types.html) describes how to define the propositional connectives.

/-
Lean's core notation
-/ /- Exercise === Show -/
-- start using Lean's propositions

/-
Notation
===

Lean defines notation like `ÔłĘ` and `Ôłž` for logic to make it look like math.
We won't redo that entire infrastructure here.
But to give a sense of it, here is how Lean defines infix
notation for `Or`, `And`, and `Not` notation.

```hs
infixr:30 " ÔłĘ "  => Or
infixr:35 " Ôłž "   => And
infixr:50 " Ôćö "   => Iff
notation:max "┬Č" p:40 => Not p
```

The numbers define the precedence of the operations. So `v` has lower precedence than `Ôłž`,
which has lower precedence than `┬Č`.

Now we can write: -/

--hide

/-
If and only iff
===

If and only if is defined inductively as
```lean
structure Iff (p q : Prop) : Prop where
  intro ::
  mp : p Ôćĺ q
  mpr : q Ôćĺ p
```

with notation `p Ôćö q`.

For example:

-/

False Elimination
===

To define the elimination rule for `False`
```
           ╬ô ÔŐó ÔŐą
  ÔŐą-Elim ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö
           ╬ô ÔŐó p
```
we take advantage of the fact that `False` was defined inductively. -/

False is Inductive
===

Finally, we have `False`, which has no introduction rule, kind of like `Empty`,
except we add the requirement that `False` is also type of `Prop`.  -/

/-
True is Inductive
===
`True` is defined inductively as
```lean
inductive True : Prop where
  | intro : True
```

for example:

Which says, given we have a proof `hp` of `p`, we need show `p`.
This is easy, we just use `hp` itself.


Aside: def, theorem, example, lemma
===

By the CHI, note that `def` and `theorem`
are essentially the same from a type theory point of few. And `example` is
just a definition without a name.

So in the above we could write:

/-
Or Elimination
===

Recall the inference rule
```none
           ╬ô,p ÔŐó r    ╬ô,q ÔŐó r    ╬ô ÔŐó p ÔłĘ q
  ÔłĘ-Elim ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö
                       ╬ô ÔŐó r
```

It allows us to prove `r` given proofs that `p Ôćĺ r`, `q Ôćĺ r` and `p ÔłĘ q`.

We can define this rule in Lean with: -/

```none
       ╬ô,¤ć ÔŐó ¤ć
```

Or is Inductive

To introduce new `Or` propositions, we use the two introduction rules

```none
                 ╬ô ÔŐó p                              ╬ô ÔŐó p
 ÔłĘ-Intro-Left ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö          ÔłĘ-Intro-Right ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö
               ╬ô ÔŐó p ÔłĘ q                          ╬ô ÔŐó p ÔłĘ q
```

Structures
===
With Lean's `And` defined as a structure we can do

/- You can view `match` as a generic elimination rule. -/


/-
Lean's And
===

Lean's And is actually defined as a structure:
```lean
structure And (a b : Prop) : Prop where
  intro ::
  left : a
  right : b
```

The `intro ::` part renames the introduction rule `intro` instead of the default `mk`.

Lean defines infix notation `Ôłž`. So you can write

-/

--hide

/-
Match is Enough
===

The elimination rules above are a _convenience_ we defined to make the proof look
more like propositional logic. We could also have written: -/

/-
Proofs with And-Elimination
===

With these inference rules, we can do more proofs: -/

/-
And Elimination
===

The elimination rules for `And` are
```none
                ╬ô ÔŐó p Ôłž q                          ╬ô ÔŐó p Ôłž q
  Ôłž-Elim-Left ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö         Ôłž-Elim-Right ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö
                  ╬ô ÔŐó p                              ╬ô ÔŐó q
```
which we can write in Lean as -/
/- And we define some variables to use throughout. -/

/- You can think of `h : And p q` as
- `h` has type `And p q`
- `h` is evidence that the type `And p q` is not empty
- `h` is a proof of the proposition `And p q`.

Proof of a Simple Proposition
===

Consider the proposition
```lean
q Ôćĺ p Ôćĺ And p q
```

As a type, this proposition is a function from `q` to `p` to `And p q`.
Thus, we know that an element of this type has the form
```lean
fun hq => fun hp => sorry
```

For the body of this lambda abstraction, we need to *introduce* an `And` type,
which requires proofs of `q` and `p` respectively. Using the inductive definition of `And` we get
```lean
fun hq hp => And.intro hp hq
```

The complete proof is then:
-/

/-
And is an Inductive Type
===

Recall the inference rule
```none
              ╬ô ÔŐó p   ╬ô ÔŐó q
    Ôłž-Intro ÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇöÔÇö
                ╬ô ÔŐó p Ôłž q
```

It states that whenever we know propositions `p` and `q`, then we know `p Ôłž q`.
From the point of view of types,
it says that if `p` and `q` are of type `Prop`, then so is `p Ôłž q`.

We can write this as an inductive type definition as follows. -/
-- goal for the `sorry` is `p`

/-
**`Ôćĺ-Elim` is lambda application:** When you see function (with type `A Ôćĺ B`) in a context
you can apply it to get a simpler goal.
-/

/-
Propositional Connectives
===

Overview
===
Inductive types capture all of propositional logic, first order logic, and more.

Instead of defining _and_, _or_ and the other logical connectives as
built-in operators in CIC, they are just defined terms of more primitive inductive types.

In this slide deck we redefine the connectives, to understand how they work.
To avoid naming conflicts with Lean's standard library, we open a namespace.
-/
-- goal for the `sorry` is `p`

/-
Implication in Lean
===

**`Ôćĺ-Intro` is lambda abstraction:** Whenever you see a goal of the form `A Ôćĺ B`, you
write a lambda to get a simpler goal.
-/
